![The Future of AI in Finance](https://files.blueprism.com/uploads/thumbnails/mo-0319_440x303px-Web-Resources.png)

### Blog Contents

New possibilities for financial institutions — that’s what artificial intelligence (AI) is delivering and will continue to deliver in the future. These possibilities impact areas like new revenue streams, a better way to operate, a more solid competitive advantage and new avenues to improve customer experience, just to name a few.

Let’s explore what the future of AI holds for financial firms and institutions, informed by our “[2025 AI, Automation and Orchestration Trends](/resources/white-papers/ai-automation-and-orchestration-trends/)” e-book and analyst insights.

## What’s the future role of AI in financial services?

Financial institutions have already seen the return on investment (ROI) [financial services automation](https://www.blueprism.com/solutions/industry/financial-services-automation/) has proved in the past years. And we’re seeing the same trajectory for AI in the finance industry.

Currently, financial institutions are using [business AI](https://www.blueprism.com/guides/ai/business-ai/) primarily to enhance customer service and experience, improve anti-money laundering efforts, and automate front- and back-office tasks. However, as the technology advances, its applications will likely expand beyond supportive add-ons with the potential to operate nearly autonomously in various areas, such as support investment strategies.

In other words, organizations will not just be leveraging AI as a one-off technology. It will be central to strategies and operations — integral to smart financial organizations — and work with other [intelligent automation](/guides/intelligent-automation/) (IA) technologies in other areas of the organization.

By 2028, 75% of enterprises that establish an AI platform strategy built on a foundation of connecting processes to broader business functions will achieve enhanced value from their investments

IDC

![Financial Services AI Agentic Survey](https://files.blueprism.com/uploads/banner-ads/_800xAUTO_fit_center-center_75_none/kl-0240-FS-Survey-linkedin-1200x627.png)

## What is the future of AI and automation in financial services?

We can't talk about AI without mentioning automation. The future will likely see these two technologies working together in a highly synergistic way. What do we mean by this?

All technologies have their specialism and values. For example, AI and generative AI are good at identifying context and understanding language from unstructured data. But no tech does it all and no tech should do so in a silo.

![](/assets/icons/quotes-blue.svg)

Automation is the power behind the throne that unlocks the vault of financial data — converting massive, unstructured information repositories from static archives into dynamic, predictive intelligence engines that can generate insights in the blink of an eye.

Zeynep Hizir

Director, SS&C Financial Services Limited

Automation is the power behind the throne that unlocks the vault of financial data — converting massive, unstructured information repositories from static archives into dynamic, predictive intelligence engines that can generate insights in the blink of an eye.

Zeynep Hizir

Director, SS&C Financial Services Limited

To fully use gen AI and AI’s capabilities, the finance industry will likely turn to an [Enterprise AI](/guides/ai/enterprise-ai/) solution: automation, orchestration and AI technologies together. Gartner now refers to this combination as Business Orchestration and Automation Technologies (BOAT).

![](/assets/icons/quotes-blue.svg)

“Orchestration is the differentiator in generative AI; it's not about individual AI capabilities but how seamlessly we can integrate intelligent systems across research, risk, client management and strategic planning to create a unified, adaptive financial intelligence platform.”

Jeremy Mackinlay

Senior marketing manager, SS&C Blue Prism

“Orchestration is the differentiator in generative AI; it's not about individual AI capabilities but how seamlessly we can integrate intelligent systems across research, risk, client management and strategic planning to create a unified, adaptive financial intelligence platform.”

Jeremy Mackinlay

Senior marketing manager, SS&C Blue Prism

![Financial Services AI Agentic Survey](https://files.blueprism.com/uploads/banner-ads/_800xAUTO_fit_center-center_75_none/kl-0240-FS-Survey-linkedin-1200x627.png)

## AI’s Impact on the Finance Industry

Here’s why organizations are betting on AI as the future of finance — exploring its current benefits, use cases and potential applications.

### What are the benefits of using AI in finance?

Here are a few key benefits of financial AI:

## What can AI do in financial services?

Applying these benefits, we can truly see [what AI can do.](/guides/ai/what-can-ai-do/) And with automation and orchestration added, even more possibilities are unlocked. From transforming financial customer services to providing personalized financial advice. Here are a three use cases:

### *P*rocess credit agreements faster

SS&C Technologies combined generative AI with digital workers to process credit agreements 95% faster. The combination allowed digital workers, who can’t normally interpret unstructured data, to understand the documents. Digital workers download the file and send it to a gen AI tool with prompts. The gen AI tool parses through the info and returns an answer that the digital worker can validate, make necessary changes and enter them into the correct fields.

[Read the full story](/resources/case-studies/ssc-ai-lending-loan-unstructured-data/)

### *Help improve customer experiences*

Danica Pension, a part of Danske Bank Group, have been active users of intelligent automation (IA) and digital workers since 2016. They’re now looking to augment their digital workforce with AI to squeeze more efficiency from them. Soon, generative AI will help them access all types of data and better understanding of their customers’ behaviors. They’ll use these AI systems to help advise customers and prevent fraudulent activities.

[Read the full story](/resources/case-studies/danica-pensions-ai-aml-due-diligence/)

### *Streamline AI equity research*

Equity researchers can save valuable time by automatically generating equity research reports based on large data sets with cutting-edge technologies, including gen AI and predictive AI. Digital workers and AI can pull reports, export charts and synthetize the data when prompted by researchers. Researchers can also outline sections of the report or key questions they need answers to, and the AI will provide insights based on the compiled research.

[See the full demo](/resources/blog/generative-ai-equity-research-langchain/)

Explore more [RPA and AI use cases in financial services](/resources/blog/ia-rpa-use-cases-in-banking-financial-services/).

## Challenges of Implementing AI in Finance

AI models need good data and oversight to ensure everything is working correctly. The technology is still developing and has highlighted a few challenges, including:

These challenges show the importance of careful AI planning and deployment — prompting financial organizations to explore an enterprise-grade solution that provides guardrails by combining AI-powered orchestration and orchestration with other technologies.

By 2028, organizations that implement comprehensive AI governance platforms will experience 40% fewer AI-related ethical incidents compared to those without such systems.

Gartner

## What Is the Future of AI in Financial Services?

How will financial organizations apply AI across their entire organization and with customers? Here are a few trends and predictions:

### 1. Personalized financial services

**Prediction:** Financial services will use AI to create hyperpersonalized offers.

Moving from a reactive stance to a proactive strategy with data (including non-financial information) for intuitive and responsive help. Eventually, AI can even anticipate and address customer needs, freeing employees from gathering all this manual data and finding areas to act on.

![Financial Services AI Agentic Survey](https://files.blueprism.com/uploads/banner-ads/_800xAUTO_fit_center-center_75_none/kl-0240-FS-Survey-linkedin-1200x627.png)
![](/assets/icons/quotes-blue.svg)

AI is ready to support client services teams in personalized investment reporting, client communication drafts, RFP creation and performance narratives – liberating teams to do far more with their time.

Brian Halpin

SVP, SS&C Blue Prism

AI is ready to support client services teams in personalized investment reporting, client communication drafts, RFP creation and performance narratives – liberating teams to do far more with their time.

Brian Halpin

SVP, SS&C Blue Prism

### 2. Unstructured data analysis

**Prediction:** Data management will continue to become more efficient.

[Gen AI](/guides/ai/generative-ai/) opens even more opportunities for data to be managed better. Gen AI can help read unstructured data from emails, documents and multimedia. It can then transform this info into actionable and structured insights that can continue being used in traditional automation.

![](/assets/icons/quotes-blue.svg)

Next year, gen AI-powered investigation tools will start to collapse multi-week money laundering investigations into hours, generating comprehensive risk narratives and connection maps that reveal hidden transactional patterns invisible to traditional human analysis.

Rob Paisley

Director, SS&C Blue Prism

Next year, gen AI-powered investigation tools will start to collapse multi-week money laundering investigations into hours, generating comprehensive risk narratives and connection maps that reveal hidden transactional patterns invisible to traditional human analysis.

Rob Paisley

Director, SS&C Blue Prism

### 3. Agentic AI

**Prediction:** Fully autonomous support alongside employees.

[Agentic AI](/guides/ai/agentic-ai/) is still in its infancy, but it promises an AI that can take actions and make decisions without requiring human intervention. For now, the prediction is AI-powered agents that work with people to enhance business processes and decision-making, with human-in-the-loop verification.

![Financial Services AI Agentic Survey](https://files.blueprism.com/uploads/banner-ads/_800xAUTO_fit_center-center_75_none/kl-0240-FS-Survey-linkedin-1200x627.png)
![](/assets/icons/quotes-blue.svg)

Agents often approach work in a non-deterministic way, and agentic capabilities are going to mature over time. As such, we’re likely to see the agents augment workflows rather than completely transform them. Companies will begin to replace some steps in their workflows to be handled by agents, likely with oversight from humans.

Omid Hosseinitabar

Senior product manager, SS&C Blue Prism

Agents often approach work in a non-deterministic way, and agentic capabilities are going to mature over time. As such, we’re likely to see the agents augment workflows rather than completely transform them. Companies will begin to replace some steps in their workflows to be handled by agents, likely with oversight from humans.

Omid Hosseinitabar

Senior product manager, SS&C Blue Prism

### 4. Wider access to financial services

**Prediction:** AI will make it more accessible and efficient to access services.

Gen AI and AI will make sophisticated investing techniques available to almost anyone. It can help perform equity research, analyze financial reports, understand market share and compile potential risks. While not perfect tools, they’re getting better. It opens opportunities for smaller, individual investors to access sophisticated techniques once only available to larger financial organizations.

![](/assets/icons/quotes-blue.svg)

Generative AI will democratize sophisticated financial planning, turning personalized, high-touch wealth management from a luxury service into a scalable, accessible solution.

Zeynep Hizir

Director, SS&C Financial Services Limited

Generative AI will democratize sophisticated financial planning, turning personalized, high-touch wealth management from a luxury service into a scalable, accessible solution.

Zeynep Hizir

Director, SS&C Financial Services Limited

## The Future of AI in Finance

Financial institutions are still in the early stages of implementing new leading AI technologies like gen AI and agentic AI, understandably aware of its challenges. But where it has been implementing AI and gen AI has seen great benefits like improved efficiency, cost optimization and personalization. It’s only a matter of time before these solutions are capable of more.

Download our AI trends and predictions e-book for more details.

Senior marketing manager, SS&C Blue Prism

Read Time: 11 minutes

FULL CONTENT:
![citizens commercial](/assets/CB_resources/images/cbds-logos/logo-citizens__horz-deepGreen.svg)

# 2025 AI Trends in Financial Management

How CFOs are using AI for payments and fraud detection

![decorative hero image](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/5bb64056-dbc7-4dff-be02-b16a0117db41/25_Business_Banking_Employees_Downtime_240404_8522_hero-commercial-retina.jpg)

[Download a print-friendly version of this article](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/de1c1b9a-0cef-4209-96b3-b230011ff832/2025_AI_Trends_Report_Original%20file.pdf)

Clear use cases for AI are emerging. The 2025 survey on AI in midsize U.S. companies provides new data on how company CFOs and PE leaders are using AI for payments, cash flow forecasting, and customer support, revealing their progress to date in putting these breakthrough technologies to use.

From payment automation to generative code-writing for due diligence, financial leaders are notching some "wins" on the board. Yet, enthusiasm could be waning across some users, and the onus is increasingly on CFOs alone to champion and implement AI across financial processes. Perception of legal risks has also climbed. Here's how CFOs and PE firms deploy AI in finance functions today and what they are planning for the future.

## Key insights

## Onus for AI in financial processes falls on CFOs

At midsize U.S. companies, CFOs are leading the way in efforts to apply AI to financial processes. While this was also true in the [2024 survey](/corporate-finance/insights/artificial-intelligence-trends-report.aspx) of AI trends, the new data paints an even clearer picture of CFOs as the primary driver of these efforts. Eight in 10 CFOs say they are in charge of promoting or implementing AI in their department.

In contrast, the 2024 survey suggested that there was more shared leadership across tech-oriented roles including chief technology officers (CTOs) and chief information officers (CIOs), and even from chief development officers (CDOs), who typically have responsibility for fundraising and external financial activities. The 2025 survey suggests that AI leadership is consolidating, for better or worse - and CFOs could be assuming more responsibility for strategizing and implementing AI in their departments.

![CFOs are taking the lead on AI for finance - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/08314951-8248-4b8c-a76b-b21f00ddfc2e/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-01_Original%20file.jpg)

![CFOs are taking the lead on AI for finance - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/08314951-8248-4b8c-a76b-b21f00ddfc2e/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-01_Original%20file.jpg)

*"We are at a pivotal point in the cycle of this technology, where firms are really starting to figure how to use AI for the biggest efficiency gains and key processes in their organizations."*

![Swamy Krish Headshot](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/1826c68f-6fe8-4d33-bdb1-b22600f6d707/Swamy_Krish_testimonial-r.jpg)

Krish Swamy

EVP Chief Data and Analytics Officer

## Use cases with the biggest impact

Payment automation is ranked as the most productive use for AI in financial processes so far. 63% of CFOs say AI has made their payment automation significantly easier, a 23% increase from 2024. Fraud detection has also proven to be a valuable use case for CFOs, with nearly six in 10 saying that AI has made fraud detection significantly easier. Cash flow forecasting also remains a popular use case in finance departments.

![Top AI Use Case Include Payment Automation and Fraud Detection - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/031810e4-2d33-4ca4-a7ea-b21f00ddfd60/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-02_Original%20file.jpg)

Private equity (PE) firms that invest in midsize companies have also eagerly embraced AI for financial processes. Since PE firms are company investors rather than company operators, their use cases differ from those used by company CFOs. Portfolio monitoring, the ongoing process of tracking and managing the performance of a firm's investments, continues to be a top use for AI, with more than half of PE firms saying the technology made it significantly easier in the past year. Many PE firms report high value in AI across a number of other processes, including exit strategies, due diligence, and investment analysis.

While adoption for AI remains high among PE firms, there was a noticeable retreat from 2024 levels. PE firms were early adopters of AI to streamline and enhance financial processes. Their waning enthusiasm could be a sign that the marginal gains of AI are diminishing with its longer use, or it could be that the reality of deploying AI is tougher than expected. It could also be driven by other causes.

![Portfolio Monitoring is the Top Financial Use for PE Firms - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/89cadc30-cf5c-4e5d-bd2c-b21f00ddfdf3/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-03_Original%20file.jpg)

![Portfolio Monitoring is the Top Financial Use for PE Firms - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/89cadc30-cf5c-4e5d-bd2c-b21f00ddfdf3/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-03_Original%20file.jpg)

Company CFOs and PE firms were both less enthusiastic about the benefits of AI for risk assessment, though many respondents submitted comments about AI's value in error-proofing processes that are prone to human mistakes. Still, there could be some more global functionality in risk management that AI tools are not yet suited to tackle. The risks of the future are not necessarily the same as the risks of the past, which could make AI a weak practitioner since it is all trained on past data.

## Use of generative AI is high, and varied

Generative AI, which can create content including text, video, pictures and music, is a narrower category of AI functionality - and it is being used by 100% of CFOs and PE respondents, according to the 2025 survey.

However, uses for generative AI differ from the broader set of AI functions. PE firms say they use generative AI most commonly for customer service, which could encompass chatbot functionalities, and for cybersecurity. CFOs at midsize companies say they are using generative AI the most for predictive analytics and cybersecurity.

![PE Firms are Using Generative AI More Broadly than Midsize Companies - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/f32a9ea3-da6f-4251-afa0-b21f00ddf7f4/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-04_Original%20file.jpg)

![PE Firms are Using Generative AI More Broadly than Midsize Companies - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/f32a9ea3-da6f-4251-afa0-b21f00ddf7f4/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-04_Original%20file.jpg)

## Perception of legal risks grows

As AI has become ubiquitous in business uses, perception of the technology has evolved - not always in the most positive way. The 2025 survey revealed an increase in the perception that AI will require significant effort to identify legal and appropriate use cases, as 92% agree. Likewise, it's more commonly agreed that AI will face significant legal barriers concerning data security, a view held by 63% of midsize company CFOs and PE firms. The 2025 survey also revealed a 10% gain in the share of respondents who think AI will make contact center jobs obsolete.

Other perceptions of AI remained more stable, including a broadly held viewpoint that AI will make it easier for stakeholders to find information, that it will become essential to business operations, and that it will generate continued cost savings.

While many are confident about AI's value, a significant (and growing) proportion say that AI will not have a significant effect on business in the next five years, a view shared by 48% of survey respondents.

![More See Legal Risks for Data Security and Use Cases - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/415d8b36-d13b-4bfd-878d-b21f00ddf91e/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-05_Original%20file.jpg)

![More See Legal Risks for Data Security and Use Cases - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/415d8b36-d13b-4bfd-878d-b21f00ddf91e/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-05_Original%20file.jpg)

## AI budgets to increase at companies and PE firms

The 2025 survey suggests a maturing understanding of an emerging technology, with more clearly defined use cases and perhaps more clearly defined expectations among financial leaders.

Among PE firms in the midsize company space, the data reflects a moderate retreat from the enthusiasm levels of 2024. Still, overall confidence in the value of AI for financial processes remains high. While spending intentions are ambitious for the five years ahead, PE firms plan to invest slightly less compared to the 2024 survey.

Midsize companies have caught up to PE firms in their confidence in the technology, and they are embracing AI investments in their budgets at a higher level in 2025 than they did in 2024. Across both groups, about seven in 10 plan to increase their current investment in AI over the next five years.

![Expected Investment in AI to Increase for Midsize Companies and PE Firms - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/914d119c-048b-4704-84e7-b21f00ddf9e8/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-06_Original%20file.jpg)

![Expected Investment in AI to Increase for Midsize Companies and PE Firms - chart infographic](https://p1.aprimocdn.net/citizensbank/914d119c-048b-4704-84e7-b21f00ddf9e8/14188_01_CIT_AI_Pulse_Web_Assets_v2-06_Original%20file.jpg)

## Actions to consider in 2025

## Explanation of methodology

Citizens worked with research firm Escalent to produce a survey that shows how financial decision-makers at mid-size companies and PE firms are thinking about AI processes and incorporating the technology in their financial operations to increase efficiency. Participants were screened for their current use of AI or their openness to using AI in the future, and those who were not using it or not interested were excluded.

# AI integration in financial services: a systematic review of trends and regulatory challenges

[*Humanities and Social Sciences Communications*](/palcomms)
**volume 12**, Article number: 562 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

43k Accesses

2 Citations

57 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into financial services represents a developmental shift in the industry, presenting unprecedented opportunities and challenges. This scientometric review examines the evolution of AI in finance from 1989 to 2024, analyzing its pivotal applications in credit scoring, fraud detection, digital insurance, robo-advisory services, and financial inclusion. The analysis reveals significant trends, particularly the growing adoption of machine learning, natural language processing, and blockchain technologies in reshaping financial operations and decision-making processes. The review addresses critical regulatory and ethical challenges, emphasizing the imperative for explainable AI (XAI) and robust governance frameworks to ensure transparency, fairness, and accountability in AI-driven systems. Despite rapid advancements, persistent gaps remain, the most notable of which is the lack of standardized frameworks for AI implementation across financial sectors. The findings support the need for a balanced approach that promotes innovation while addressing ethical, regulatory, and societal concerns. This comprehensive synthesis maps the trajectory of AI in finance, identifies key areas for future research, and recommends interdisciplinary collaboration to advance responsible and sustainable AI integration within the financial ecosystem.

## Introduction

Artificial Intelligence (AI) has emerged as a disruptive force in modern finance and has almost completely overhauled how operations are carried out in the industry (Tao et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR167 "Tao R, Su C-W, Xiao Y, Dai K, Khalid F (2021) Robo advisors, algorithmic trading and investment management: Wonders of fourth industrial revolution in financial markets. Technol Forecast Social Change 163:120421")). AI, which typically involves technologies such as machine learning, deep learning, and natural language processing, now dictates the mediums for financial functions. Its impact cuts across a wide range of applications—from algorithmic trading and fraud detection to customer service chatbots and robo-advisors (Ranković et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR155 "Ranković M, Gurgu E, Martins OMD, Vukasović’ M (2023) Artificial Intelligence and the Evolution of Finance: Opportunities, Challenges and Ethical Considerations. EdTech Journal. Retrieved from 
                  https://doi.org/10.18485/edtech.2023.3.1.2
                  
                ")).

This rise in adoption is also evident in the projected doubling of financial institutions’ AI expenditure, expected to reach $97 billion by 2027 (Kearns, [2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR96 "Kearns J (2023) AI’s reverberations across finance. IMF. Retrieved from 
                  https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/fandd/issues/2023/12/AI-reverberations-across-finance-Kearns#:~:text=For%20other%20uses%2C%20such%20as,%2C%20ethical%2C%20and%20compliant%20framework
                  
                ")). With an estimated compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 29.6%, the financial sector is now the fastest-growing industry globally in terms of AI investment (La Croce, [2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR105 "La Croce C (2023) Genai solutions help European AI market thrive in an uncertain economic environment, says IDC. IDC: The Premier Global Market Intelligence Company. Retrieved from 
                  https://www.idc.com/getdoc.jsp?containerId=prEUR251222623
                  
                ")). This exponential growth has prompted leading financial firms such as JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley Wealth Management to establish their AI infrastructures, recognizing the technology’s transformative potential (Kearns, [2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR96 "Kearns J (2023) AI’s reverberations across finance. IMF. Retrieved from 
                  https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/fandd/issues/2023/12/AI-reverberations-across-finance-Kearns#:~:text=For%20other%20uses%2C%20such%20as,%2C%20ethical%2C%20and%20compliant%20framework
                  
                ")). However, this transformative potential presents a paradox: while AI is capable of driving breakthrough performances, it also harbors systemic risks that depends primarily on how it is regulated and ethically deployed (Ahern, [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR2 "Ahern D (2021) Regulatory lag, regulatory friction and regulatory transition as fintech disenablers: Calibrating an EU response to the regulatory sandbox phenomenon. Eur Bus Organ Law Rev 22(3):395–432"); Arner et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR14 "Arner DW, Buckley RP, Zetzsche DA, Veidt R (2020) Sustainability, FinTech and Financial Inclusion. Eur Bus Organ Law Rev 21(1):7–35. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s40804-020-00183-y
                  
                "); Berdiyeva et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR25 "Berdiyeva O, Islam MU, Saeedi M (2021) Artificial intelligence in accounting and finance: Meta-analysis. Int Bus Rev 3(1):56–79")).

Recent developments in advanced AI models, such as ChatGPT and DeepSeek, reinforce the argument for the immense benefits that can be derived from AI technologies; however, their cross-border and pervasive nature also introduces novel risks that demand careful scrutiny to prevent potential widespread crises (Bahoo et al., [2024](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR20 "Bahoo S, Cucculelli M, Goga X, Mondolo J (2024) Artificial intelligence in finance: A comprehensive review through bibliometric and content analysis. SN Bus Econ 4(2):23")). One such critical risk is the issue of human oversight. Effective oversight requires that human decision-makers possess the ability to interpret and evaluate AI-generated outputs, to accept, reject, or modify AI recommendations based on ethical, legal, and practical considerations (Černevičienė & Kabašinskas, [2024](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR36 "Černevičienė J, Kabašinskas A (2024) Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) in finance: a systematic literature review. Artif Intell Rev 57(8):216")). This ensures that ultimate responsibility remains with human operators, who can intervene to mitigate adverse outcomes and align AI applications with ethical standards. Such oversight not only ensures accountability but also enhances the responsible use of AI technologies, guarding against risks while fostering trust in AI-driven financial systems (Max et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR130 "Max R, Kriebitz A, Von Websky C (2021) Ethical considerations about the implications of artificial intelligence in finance. In: San-Jose L, Retolaza J, van Liedekerke L (eds) Handbook on ethics in finance (pp. 577–592) Springer, Cham")).

As AI in finance continues through its adoption and growth phase, it is expected that its full benefits and potential threats will become more apparent over time. This dynamic has spurred a significant surge in AI finance publications in recent years, as researchers strive to address literature gaps and identify emerging trajectories to advance the field (Goodell et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR72 "Goodell JW, Kumar S, Lahmar O, Pandey N (2023) A bibliometric analysis of cultural finance. Int Rev Financ Anal 85:102442")). Over the past two decades, the volume of publications has risen considerably, from an annual average of 29 to 178 articles, based on our dataset. These studies explore a wide range of topics, including optimal financial models, associated risks, and diverse applications across various facets of finance.

Since the early 1990s, when scientific research on AI in finance first emerged, numerous technologies have been adopted, redefined or replaced in response to the evolving needs of financial markets. Concurrently, the terminology and focus of research have shifted to mirror this changing landscape (Leone & de Medeiros, [2015](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR113 "Leone V, de Medeiros OR (2015) Signalling the dotcom bubble: A multiple changes in persistence approach. Q Rev Econ Financ 55:77–86")). For researchers and finance professionals, understanding both foundational and niche themes in AI is crucial to developing technologies and research that align with current trends and dynamics. Against this backdrop, this study examines trends in AI finance research to identify key stakeholders, influential topics, and areas that are prime for further exploration to provide a structured analysis of research gaps and development trajectories.

Major research gaps remain in the literature, particularly in understanding the evolving regulatory landscape and ethical considerations surrounding AI-based finance (Brummer and Gorfine). The fast pace of AI application adoption demands that current regulatory frameworks and ethical dilemmas are critically examined, including issues of algorithmic bias and fairness (Friedler et al., [2019](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR63 "Friedler SA, Scheidegger C, Venkatasubramanian S, Choudhary S, Hamilton EP, Roth D (2019) A comparative study of fairness-enhancing interventions in machine learning. In Proceedings of the Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, p. 329–338")). Addressing these issues is essential to ensuring the responsible development and deployment of AI technologies in finance and protecting the interests of both financial institutions and consumers (Pithadia, [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR152 "Pithadia HJ (2021) Algorithmic Regulation using AI and Blockchain Technology (Unpublished doctoral dissertation)")).

Regulators face considerable challenges in understanding the underlying mechanisms of complex AI systems, complicating their efforts to establish effective oversight. Similarly, consumers struggle to decipher the reasoning behind AI-generated outputs in their decision-making process. These challenges led to the emergence of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI), which prioritizes transparency and interpretability in AI models (Chen et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR41 "Chen X-Q, Ma C-Q, Ren Y-S, Lei Y-T, Huynh NQA, Narayan S (2023) Explainable artificial intelligence in finance: A bibliometric review. Financ Res Lett 56:104145. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.frl.2023.104145
                  
                ")). Moreover, the rapid pace of AI technology advancements implies that regulatory frameworks are continuously updated, which imposes substantial costs on regulatory bodies. Other regulatory challenges include ambiguous regulations (Arner, [2019](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR15 "Arner DW (2019) The evolution of fintech: A new post-crisis paradigm? In The Evolution of Fintech: A New Post-Crisis Paradigm?: Arner, Douglas W. [Sl]: SSRN")), data privacy and security concerns (Lopez & Alcaide, [2020](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR120 "Lopez BS, Alcaide A (2020) Blockchain, AI and IoT to improve governance, financial management and control of crisis: Case study COVID-19")), and the lack of global regulatory harmonization (Erdélyi & Goldsmith, [2018](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR59 "Erdélyi OJ, Goldsmith J (2018) Regulating artificial intelligence: Proposal for a global solution. In Proceedings of the 2018 AAAI/ACM Conference on AI, Ethics, and Society p 95–101")) —factors that collectively threaten the effective and ethical use of AI in finance. The 2008 financial crisis—triggered by lax oversight, complex financial products and inadequate risk assessment—serve as a stark reminder of the consequences of regulatory failures (Vukovic et al., [2019](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR174 "Vukovic D, Lapshina KA, Maiti M (2019) European Monetary Union bond market dynamics: Pre & post crisis. Res Int Bus Financ 50:369–380"); Gorton & Metrick, [2012](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR75 "Gorton G, Metrick A (2012) Securitized banking and the run on repo. J Financ Econ 104(3):425–451")). The 2010 Flash Crash also further exemplifies the risks posed by inadequately regulated AI-driven systems in causing sudden and severe market disruptions (Frömmel, [2022](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR64 "Frömmel M (2022) International financial markets in the digital era. In Digitalization and the Future of Financial Services: Innovation and Impact of Digital Finance. Springer, p 85–101")).

This study addresses these critical gaps through a comprehensive literature review, employing a scientometric approach to analyze the existing body of research on AI in finance. The primary objectives are twofold: (1) to identify prevailing research trends and prospects in AI finance, and (2) to investigate the applications, regulatory frameworks, and ethical considerations associated with AI in finance. The scientometric methodology provides a robust, objective framework for analyzing trends and identifying gaps in AI-based finance research. Through this approach, the study makes several novel contributions to literature. First, it employs a larger and more recent dataset to provide an up-to-date perspective on AI in finance developments. Second, it analyzes evolving trends in AI techniques, offering insights into the field’s technological progression, influential contributors, and potential areas for research (Bahoo et al., [2024](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR20 "Bahoo S, Cucculelli M, Goga X, Mondolo J (2024) Artificial intelligence in finance: A comprehensive review through bibliometric and content analysis. SN Bus Econ 4(2):23")). Third, by examining regulatory frameworks and ethical considerations, the study provides a guiding framework for responsible AI integration in finance. These insights are crucial for promoting innovative financial technology, robust governance standards and enhancing trust in AI-driven financial systems.

The next parts of this paper are structured as follows: Section 2 reviews the relevant literature, to provide a foundation for understanding the key themes and developments. Section 3 outlines the research methodology to describe the data sources and analytical approaches employed in this study. Section 4 presents the main findings, combining scientometric and content analyses to reveal trends and patterns in AI finance research. Section 5 delves into the regulation of AI in finance, synthesizing critical studies and highlighting key gaps. Finally, Section 6 concludes the paper by discussing the implications of the findings and proposing avenues for future research.

## Literature background

### Artificial intelligence in finance: evolution, impact, and regulatory perspectives

Early literature conceptualized AI primarily as a tool for automation. However, with the introduction of advanced algorithms and computational models, AI has evolved into a more comprehensive tool in recent studies (Johnson et al., [2019](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR94 "Johnson K, Pasquale F, Chapman J (2019) Artificial intelligence, machine learning, and bias in finance: Toward responsible innovation. 88 Fordham Law Review. Retrieved from 
                  https://ir.lawnet.fordham.edu/flr/vol88/iss2/5
                  
                "); Arslanian & Fischer, [2019](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR16 "Arslanian H, Fischer F (2019) The future of finance: The impact of fintech, AI, and crypto on financial services. Springer Nature Switzerland AG")). This evolution has resulted in the development of several theoretical frameworks for understanding AI’s role in finance. The mechanistic viewpoint focuses on AI’s capacity for automating routine tasks through rule-based systems to streamline operational efficiency within financial institutions. In contrast, the predictive analytics viewpoint highlights AI’s ability to support market analysis and decision-making, particularly through machine learning applications (Wang et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR179 "Wang Q, Kang K, Zhang Z, Cao D (2021). Application of LSTM and Conv1D LSTM network in stock forecasting model. Bilingual Publishing Co")). These divergent conceptualizations demonstrate AI’s complexity and its varied applications across different financial domains.

The technological foundation underlying AI financial applications has evolved through distinct phases, each marked by significant advances in computing power, data availability, and algorithmic sophistication (Arner et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR14 "Arner DW, Buckley RP, Zetzsche DA, Veidt R (2020) Sustainability, FinTech and Financial Inclusion. Eur Bus Organ Law Rev 21(1):7–35. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s40804-020-00183-y
                  
                ")). Contemporary AI systems in finance are distinguished by their ability to process and analyze vast datasets in real-time, leveraging multiple technological components that work in concert. Machine learning models extract patterns from historical data, while natural language processing (NLP) algorithms decode unstructured textual information. Neural networks, designed to mimic human cognitive processes, enable these systems to handle increasingly complex analytical tasks (Zhang et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR190 "Zhang L, Pentina I, Fan Y (2021) Who do you choose? Comparing perceptions of human vs robo-advisor in the context of financial services. J Serv Mark 35(5):634–646")). This technological convergence has enabled AI to transcend its initial role in basic process automation and emerge as a sophisticated tool for financial analysis and decision-making.

The historical trajectory of AI in finance demonstrates how technological advancement has fundamentally altered financial services delivery and operations. The evolution progressed from basic rule-based automation systems in the initial stages to increasingly sophisticated applications incorporating predictive analytics and machine learning (Johnson et al., [2019](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR94 "Johnson K, Pasquale F, Chapman J (2019) Artificial intelligence, machine learning, and bias in finance: Toward responsible innovation. 88 Fordham Law Review. Retrieved from 
                  https://ir.lawnet.fordham.edu/flr/vol88/iss2/5
                  
                ")). This change was more than just technical; it represented a vital shift in how financial institutions approached data analysis, risk assessment, and decision-making processes. The progression from automated task execution to complex predictive modeling shows the technology’s expanding capabilities and its growing strategic significance in financial operations.

Contemporary developments in AI finance are marked by several interconnected trends that are reshaping industry practices. Natural Language Processing (NLP) and sentiment analysis are used to understand textual data, enabling financial institutions to gauge market sentiment and make informed investment decisions (Gao et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR67 "Gao R, Zhang Z, Shi Z, Xu D, Zhang W, Zhu D (2021) A review of natural language processing for financial technology. In International Symposium on Artificial Intelligence and Robotics 2021, vol. 11884. SPIE, p 262–277")). Explainable AI (XAI) has gained prominence as a critical factor in ensuring regulatory compliance and building user trust by making AI algorithms more interpretable (Chen et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR41 "Chen X-Q, Ma C-Q, Ren Y-S, Lei Y-T, Huynh NQA, Narayan S (2023) Explainable artificial intelligence in finance: A bibliometric review. Financ Res Lett 56:104145. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.frl.2023.104145
                  
                ")). Robotic Process Automation (RPA) is streamlining back-office operations, reducing costs, and improving efficiency (Madakam et al., [2019](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR126 "Madakam S, Holmukhe RM, Jaiswa, DK (2019) The Future Digital Work Force: Robotic Process Automation (RPA). JISTEM - Journal of Information Systems and Technology Management, 16 Retrieved from 
                  https://www.scielo.br/j/jistm/a/m7cqFWJPsWSk8ZnWRN6fR5m/
                  
                ")). Additionally, AI-driven chatbots and virtual assistants are enhancing customer interactions by providing personalized services and resolving queries more efficiently (Iovine et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR91 "Iovine A, Narducci F, Musto C, de Gemmis M, Semeraro G (2023) Virtual Customer Assistants in finance: From state of the art and practices to design guidelines. Comput Sci Rev 47:100534")). Algorithmic trading, powered by AI, is optimizing investment strategies, offering greater precision and speed in executing trades (Arner, [2019](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR15 "Arner DW (2019) The evolution of fintech: A new post-crisis paradigm? In The Evolution of Fintech: A New Post-Crisis Paradigm?: Arner, Douglas W. [Sl]: SSRN")). These trends collectively reflect the integration of different AI applications to improve the financial sector.

Looking ahead, the financial industry is set to be influenced by a number of new developments in AI. For instance, quantum computing promises to deliver unmatched computational power for complex financial modeling and optimization (Woerner & Egger, [2019](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR181 "Woerner S, Egger DJ (2019) Quantum risk analysis. npj Quantum Inf 5(1):1–8")). AI-based fraud detection systems are evolving through advanced anomaly detection algorithms to enhance security and mitigate risks (El Hajj & Hammoud, [2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR58 "El Hajj M, Hammoud J (2023) Unveiling the Influence of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning on Financial Markets: A Comprehensive Analysis of AI Applications in Trading, Risk Management, and Financial Operations. J Risk Financ Manage 16(10), 434")). Continuous advancements in neural networks and deep learning are expanding AI’s ability to analyze unstructured data, such as images and audio, for applications in fraud prevention and customer service. Augmented Intelligence, which emphasizes collaboration between humans and AI, is gaining traction as a decision-support tool in complex financial scenarios (Tao et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR167 "Tao R, Su C-W, Xiao Y, Dai K, Khalid F (2021) Robo advisors, algorithmic trading and investment management: Wonders of fourth industrial revolution in financial markets. Technol Forecast Social Change 163:120421")). Furthermore, the integration of blockchain and AI is paving the way for decentralized, transparent, and secure solutions, particularly in areas such as smart contracts and digital identity (Kshetri, [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR101 "Kshetri N (2021) The role of artificial intelligence in promoting financial inclusion in developing countries, vol. 24, Taylor & Francis")). These emerging trends highlight the ongoing evolution of AI in finance, pointing to a future where its influence will be more pervasive and transformative.

These developments of AI in finance have also been extensively studied through bibliometric approaches. Seminal works by Chen et al. ([2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR41 "Chen X-Q, Ma C-Q, Ren Y-S, Lei Y-T, Huynh NQA, Narayan S (2023) Explainable artificial intelligence in finance: A bibliometric review. Financ Res Lett 56:104145. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.frl.2023.104145
                  
                ")), Tao et al. ([2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR167 "Tao R, Su C-W, Xiao Y, Dai K, Khalid F (2021) Robo advisors, algorithmic trading and investment management: Wonders of fourth industrial revolution in financial markets. Technol Forecast Social Change 163:120421")), Goodell et al. ([2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR73 "Goodell JW, Kumar S, Lim WM, Pattnaik D (2021) Artificial intelligence and machine learning in finance: Identifying foundations, themes, and research clusters from bibliometric analysis. Journal of Behavioral and Experimental Finance, 32, 100577. Retrieved from 
                  https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S2214635021001210
                  
                
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbef.2021.100577
                  
                ")), and Ahmed et al. ([2022](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR4 "Ahmed S, Alshater MM, El Ammari A, Hammami H (2022) Artificial intelligence and machine learning in finance: A bibliometric review. Res Int Bus Financ 61:101646")) have explored the foundational elements, thematic underpinnings, and research clusters in AI literature. These studies employ techniques such as co-citation analysis, bibliometric coupling, NLP-based bibliometric approaches, and integrated CiteSpace analysis to uncover evolving research trends in AI-based finance. Chen et al. ([2023](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR41 "Chen X-Q, Ma C-Q, Ren Y-S, Lei Y-T, Huynh NQA, Narayan S (2023) Explainable artificial intelligence in finance: A bibliometric review. Financ Res Lett 56:104145. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.frl.2023.104145
                  
                ")), for instance, focus on Explainable AI (XAI) in finance, noting a significant increase in publications since 2013. Their research notes a transition from traditional finance research toward more inclusive and diversified applications, accompanied by improvements in non-interpretable models and a growing emphasis on risk and ethical considerations. Other studies also identify three principal literature clusters within AI finance: (1) portfolio construction, computation, and investor behavior; (2) financial fraud and distress; and (3) sentiment inference, forecasting, and planning. These clusters show the major applications of AI in finance (Goodell et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR73 "Goodell JW, Kumar S, Lim WM, Pattnaik D (2021) Artificial intelligence and machine learning in finance: Identifying foundations, themes, and research clusters from bibliometric analysis. Journal of Behavioral and Experimental Finance, 32, 100577. Retrieved from 
                  https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S2214635021001210
                  
                
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbef.2021.100577
                  
                ")). Ahmed et al. ([2022](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR4 "Ahmed S, Alshater MM, El Ammari A, Hammami H (2022) Artificial intelligence and machine learning in finance: A bibliometric review. Res Int Bus Financ 61:101646")) observe a surge in literature on Machine Learning (ML) and AI finance, with the United States, China, and the United Kingdom emerging as the top contributors. This global distribution of research highlights the international significance and interest in AI applications within finance, and the leading role these countries play in its exploration and development.

The global perspectives, however, vary on how AI is regulated. Efforts to address ethical concerns, regulatory gaps, and privacy issues related to AI applications have revealed divergent views across different regions, with limited literature available to quantitatively or qualitatively determine the best approaches (Lee, [2020](/articles/s41599-025-04850-8#ref-CR112 "Lee J (2020) Access to finance for artificial intelligence regulation in the financial services industry. Eur Bus Organ Law Rev 21:731–757")). The European Banking Institute advocates for robust and centralized governance to address risks and regulatory fragmentation, particularly in cross-border FinTech trade, while the United States has adopted a more decentralized approach th

enriched_points.expanded_content: AI is reshaping finance by enhancing customer service, improving fraud detection, and automating operations, making institutions more efficient and customer-focused.
Embedded finance integrates financial services into non-financial platforms, streamlining transactions and enhancing user experiences without traditional banking barriers.
Blockchain technology offers secure, transparent transactions while reducing costs and fraud, revolutionizing payment systems and smart contracts in financial services.
Green finance promotes investments in sustainable projects, driven by consumer demand for eco-friendly solutions and regulatory incentives, reshaping traditional investment strategies.
FinTech innovations influence consumer behavior by offering personalized services, increasing financial literacy, and enhancing accessibility, leading to more informed financial decisions.
Investment patterns are shifting towards technology-driven solutions and sustainable practices, reflecting a growing preference for innovative, ethical, and efficient financial opportunities.
